What will happen when an intifada like the Guardian of the Walls riots breaks out? It is clear to all that the crime/terror organizations will use attack drones against police forces and more. The Israeli government must issue an order for an operation to collect all weapons held by Arabs in Israel, and to recruit the Shin Bet for this purpose and if necessary take severe measures against those who do not hand over their weapons to the police, otherwise it will blow up in our faces one day. [Hananel Aviv]

history_headlines.headline: Seriously, you don't need to be a genius to understand the following things, the latest update regarding the nuclear situation in Iran, it's important to note that this update is accurate as of September 2025, what has happened since then 🤔 2391 kg of uranium, at an enrichment level of 2%. 6024 kg of uranium, at an enrichment level of 5%. 184 kg of uranium, at an enrichment level of 20%. 440 kg of uranium, at an enrichment level of 60%. There is a third enrichment site in Iran, which was exposed on June 12, 2025, located in Isfahan and called 'IFEP' and no one knew about its existence. Details and specifications of this site were prepared to submit to the agency in accordance with the plan as preparation for inspection, but Iran canceled its decision later due to the 12-day war. The third uranium enrichment facility in Iran and the second in Isfahan, is considered a secret facility that the international agency failed to reach, and it was also not attacked by Israel during the war, due to lack of information. Now I ask, the size of Iran is over one million six hundred thousand square kilometers, who can guarantee that there are no additional facilities or facilities that really really no one knows about? I think you already know the answer yourself. Abu Saleh's Desk Arabic

The family is facing a difficult struggle - emotional and financial - and any help can make a difference. Every donation, small or large, brings Yanai one step closer to recovery. joke: Jerry Seinfeld: You know, they’re banning kids from social networks everywhere. It’s like saying, “Hey kids, don’t even think about communicating with each other unless you’re in a group project and the teacher is monitoring you.” What’s the plan here? Are we just hoping they’ll start sending carrier pigeons to communicate? I can see it now: kids jump on their bikes, clutching messages in tiny bird backpacks, just trying to get the latest gossip while dodging traffic. Because nothing says “youthful rebellion” like a pigeon with a “Just Text Me” sticker on its side!
jokes: Jerry Seinfeld: You know, they’re banning kids from social networks everywhere. It’s like saying, “Hey kids, don’t even think about communicating with each other unless you’re in a group project and the teacher is monitoring you.” What’s the plan here? Are we just hoping they’ll start sending carrier pigeons to communicate? I can see it now: kids jump on their bikes, clutching messages in tiny bird backpacks, just trying to get the latest gossip while dodging traffic. Because nothing says “youthful rebellion” like a pigeon with a “Just Text Me” sticker on its side!

Chris Rock: So Pakistan is out there striking targets in Afghanistan, blaming them for attacks, just like that kid in school who blames the dog for eating his homework! I mean, come on! They say, "It wasn't me! It was those Afghan terrorists!" Like they’re playing tag—"You’re it!" "No, you’re it!" But here’s the kicker, folks: if your neighbors keep throwing rocks at your house, maybe it’s time to check if your own backyard is a war zone! Just a thought! Sometimes the guy pointing fingers is just mad that his finger’s got a little rock dust on it!

Jerry Seinfeld: So, it turns out the Air Force is attacking terror targets in Rafah and Gaza City. And I can’t help but wonder, when did we go from fighting wars to just sending in our own delivery service? I mean, do they arrive in high-tech drones with a little “bon appétit” note attached? And in this day and age, why are we still using planes? Can’t we just send texts to let them know we’re unhappy? “Hey, we’re really frustrated over here in the U.S. You mind taking it down a notch? Thanks!”

Suzy Izzard: Imagine a meeting between historical maps and modern geography, with maps rolling their eyes and saying, "Oh, here we go again, the Greater Land of Israel. Honestly, it’s not a buffet; you can’t just keep adding more lands to your plate! It’s like Iran’s in the corner, clutching its tea, whispering ‘What next, a land for the penguins?’ As if they’d never heard the phrase ‘less is more’—because clearly for them, ‘greater’ is a philosophy they apply to countries and maybe even snack portions! Meanwhile, the camp residents have become the world’s most reluctant game of hide and seek: “You’re it! But please, resist getting arrested or we’ll have to make this a public event!" Such commitment to the craft of conflict—it's like they think they’re auditioning for a geopolitical soap opera: "As the World Turns: The Drama of Maps!"

Mitch Hedberg: I read that the Israeli Air Force is dropping air strikes in Rafah, which sounds like an extreme version of giving someone a surprise party. “Surprise! You just got bombed. I hope you like cake!” It’s like they’re trying to spice up the neighborhood, but the only thing on the menu is chaos. I mean, how do you even RSVP to that? “Yes, I’ll be there... but I might need a helmet instead of a gift.”

**Chris Rock**: You know, I just read about this closed meeting in the White House with all these heavyweights—Trump, the Air Force Commander, Secretary of State, and they’re in there for over three hours. Man, that’s not a meeting; that’s a hostage situation! I mean, what’s the plan? Do they think if they talk long enough, someone will pull a rabbit out of a hat? Or maybe they’re just trying to figure out how to take a vacation while pretending they’re working! Meanwhile, all of us out here are just hoping someone finds that missing dead body on a hiking trail before the teens start using it for a new escape room game! Because let’s be real for a second, the only thing scarier than hiking trails is how people will argue about anything these days—now we can add hiking “mysteries” to the list!

Jerry Seinfeld: Why is it that 61% of voters are in favor of Israeli settlements in the Gaza Strip? I mean, that’s when you realize we’ve officially reached the point where people are basically like, “You know what this highly contested area needs? More people moving in!” It’s as if someone looked at a crowded subway and thought, “Ah, yes, let’s add more seats to this roller coaster of chaos!” It’s like trying to solve an overcrowding problem by inviting more friends to the party—at some point, you just have to wonder if anyone’s paying attention to the social etiquette of territorial disputes!

Sarah Silverman: So, the U.S. Ambassador to Israel, Mike Hackey, says that Israel's got the divine right to take land from the Nile to the Euphrates. You know, it's refreshing to hear someone so confident in their interpretation of God's map. I mean, forget showing me the way to success—I want to know how to navigate the divine real estate market. Meanwhile, Australia won’t even repatriate the families of ISIS fighters from Syria. So, it seems God might be fine with expansion packs, but countries are like, “Nah, we’ll just stick to our current players.” What a wild game of Monopoly where everyone’s just flipping the board and saying, “My way or the highway—and the highway’s paved with divine right!”

Sarah Silverman: So, Pakistan just attacked seven terror camps on the Afghan border, right? I mean, talk about overachievers. At this point, their tourism slogan should be, “Visit Pakistan: we do the border hopping so you don’t have to!” It’s like they’re trying to win the Olympics of unpredictability while the rest of us just sit here hoping for a nice, peaceful weekend. But hey, if they keep this up, they might even get a sponsorship from the United Nations – “Pakistan: where the only thing getting bombed are our hopes for a casual Friday!”

Sarah Silverman: So the Yemeni Houthis are raising alert levels to emergency status because they're worried the U.S. might attack Yemen while trying to intervene on behalf of Iran. It’s like they’re playing a game of “Who’s Going to Get Bombed First?” only everyone involved forgot that no one wins—especially not the civilians. Honestly, at this point, if you’re not getting alerts in Sanaa, you’re just not trying hard enough to be part of global chaos. It’s like the VIP section of a disaster movie where everybody’s too busy arguing about who gets the popcorn instead of wondering why the theater's on fire.

Chris Rock: So let me get this straight—61% of those folks are like, "Yeah, let’s go ahead and build some houses in Gaza!” Meanwhile, I can’t even get the landlord to fix my leaky faucet! Do you hear the irony? They’re out there like, "We need more homes," while I’m over here just trying to keep my home from becoming a swimming pool! It’s wild! You got a place where people are fighting each other over land like it’s a game of Monopoly, and the only thing I’m fighting for is an apartment that doesn’t smell like last week’s fish fry! You can’t make this up, man! It’s like saying, “Hey, let’s plant a garden in the middle of a tornado!”

Lewis Black: So the U.S. ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, claims that Israel can stretch its borders all the way from the Nile to the Euphrates because, get this, "God promised." Oh sure, because any time I’ve ever been late for work and asked my boss for a raise, I just waved my hands and said, “Well, God told me to!” Look, if faith and absurdity were a currency, we’d be billionaires by now! This isn’t diplomacy, folks; it’s a divine game of Red Rover with real geopolitical consequences!

**Jerry Seinfeld**: So, Yemen has raised its alert status to emergency level, but they're not telling anyone why. It's like when you walk into a room and your friends are all whispering, and you’re a little nervous—'Did I walk in on a secret mission? Am I the emergency?' You look around and think, 'You know, if the stakes are this high, can’t we at least get a newsletter or something? A polite email: "Heads up, we’re on red alert, but don’t mind us, just doing our thing!" It’s absurd!

Sarah Silverman: Isn’t it wild how ISIS is out there claiming responsibility for attacks in Syria like they’re the world's worst PR agency? I mean, if they were trying to market Syria as a "must-visit" destination, they should really consider a new slogan— "Come for the chaos, stay for the headlines!" Meanwhile, over in the Gaza Strip, they’ve got a survey showing that a staggering 61% support Israeli settlements. Apparently, nothing says “I love my country” quite like doing some real estate shopping in someone else’s backyard. It's like organizing a neighborhood barbecue in a house that’s clearly not yours and inviting everyone to join—you’ll just have to ignore those pesky “No Trespassing” signs!

Lewis Black: So, apparently, a survey reveals that 61% of people support Israeli settlements in the Gaza Strip! Look, I don't know about you, but if I were living somewhere and random folks just decided to show up and build a condo complex on my front yard, I wouldn’t be raising my hand excitedly! What do they think? It’s a timeshare? “Welcome to Gaza! You get the ocean view and free Wi-Fi! Sorry about the history, but hey, check out the amenities!” I mean, at what point does common sense pack its bags and leave for a much more sane destination?

Eddie Izzard: Ah, so we have eleven families uprooted by 'settler harassment' near the village of Al-Mughayir. It’s as if the settlers decided that the land was just one big, unoccupied couch left out by the curb, completely ignoring the fact that those families had long since adopted it, complete with their own throw pillows and a questionable wine collection, which, by the way, any historian would tell you is vital for negotiating peace in the Middle East. So, what’s next? Are we going to discuss land rights with a negotiation team made up of historical figures? Picture Julius Caesar on one side and Moses on the other, both debating who gets the prime real estate, while back at home, one of the families is just trying to get their Wi-Fi signal strong enough to stream the latest episode of a soap opera about neighboring families arguing over who stole the last pita bread. It’s the absurdity of modern life, reducing centuries of conflict to a social media hashtag battle over dinner rolls!

Chris Rock: So I’m reading about these families in Palestine being forced to leave their land because of "settler harassment." Wait a minute, harassment? Harassment ain't just a nagging phone call! You can't just call your landlord like, “Hey man, I think my lease is being violated!” These folks are out here packing up their lives while some settlers are acting like bad roommates. You know it’s bad when people are getting harassed off their own land like it’s the world’s strangest eviction notice! This ain't just uninvited guests; it’s a whole unwelcome neighborhood! Man, if you can’t afford rent, stay in your lane—but kicking people off their ancestral homes? Nah, that’s not just harassment, that’s gentrification on steroids!

Lewis Black: Imagine this! Eleven Palestinian families are forced to leave their land because of "settler harassment." Settler harassment? What is that, a Yelp review gone too far? "I just can't recommend this land anymore; the settlers were rude, they didn't even offer me a drink!" We're talking about actual people here, getting booted out like they’re in a bad game of musical chairs! It’s like bureaucrats woke up one day and thought, “You know what we need? More land disputes and less common sense!” Who needs stability when we can have chaos wrapped in a bow of “It’s just policy”?

Eddie Izzard: Imagine a group of 37,000 voters sitting around a table discussing Israeli settlements in Gaza like it’s some sort of a real estate seminar. "Ladies and gentlemen, let’s draw up plans for a new condo complex—integrated with artisanal hummus and infused with a rich history of conflict! Think of the property value! This is like if Gandalf tried to sell you a fixer-upper in Mordor—sure, there’s lava, but have you seen the mountain views?!” And 61% of folks are nodding along, utterly unaware that the 'Buy One, Get One Free' promotion includes a side of geopolitical tension. So, should we celebrate this newfound land of milk and honey, or just start planting flags like they're garden gnomes? Who knew real estate could be so hostile yet thrilling—welcome to Gaza, where the only thing getting settled is your lunch order!

Mitch Hedberg: I overheard the US Air Force is in the White House for an emergency meeting, which is weird because I thought the only thing that helps an emergency is a coffee break – or at least a good sandwich. Meanwhile, 13 Arab countries are condemning the US ambassador to Israel, and I’m over here just trying to figure out if “condemnation” is a type of new-age yoga or a recipe for regret.
